results.possibleIdentity: Herbert D. Goldman (H.D. Goldman) was an electrical engineer and educator active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He held positions as an Assistant Professor of Electrical Engineering at Hofstra University in Hempstead, New York, and was also an engineer with the Sperry Gyroscope Company (Sperry Rand) in Great Neck/Lake Success, near Bayside, New York. His technical expertise spanned digital circuits, reliability engineering, mathematics, computers, and communications television. He was a pioneer in applying error-correcting codes to digital television and memory systems, co-authoring the paper 'An Error-Correcting System for Digital Television' in 1968. He graduated from Columbia University (B.S. 1948), which accounts for his dual association with Hofstra and Columbia. Given his specialized knowledge in television and integrated circuits and his proximity to the New York art-engineering hub, he was a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that paired technical specialists with artists for collaborative projects. His work frequently appeared in IEEE publications, including the 'IEEE International Convention Record' and 'IRE Transactions on Electronic Computers'.
